title: Arachnoid Cysts -- History and Physical
content: Arachnoid cysts may cause atypical symptoms also. Bobble-head doll syndrome, a rhythmic movement of the head, has been reported due to third ventricular dilation and potentially cerebellar involvement. [32] [33] Depression that resolves with the marsupialization of an arachnoid has also occurred in some patients. The presentation of an arachnoid cyst causing depression is quite rare but plausible given the location and marked mass effect the cyst had on the brain. [34] The rupture of an arachnoid cyst resulting in a subdural hygroma has rarely been observed. The rupture can be due to trauma or can be spontaneous. Usually, the subdural hygroma can be followed without treatment, but surgical intervention is occasionally required. Furthermore, the traumatic rupture of an arachnoid cyst rarely results in hemorrhage or a subdural hematoma. [35] [36] [37]

title: Hydrocephalus -- Pearls and Other Issues
content: Hydrocephalus had complex pathogenesis and multiple causes. There are four main types of hydrocephalus: obstructive, communicating, hypersecretory, and NPH. Hydrocephalus may be classified as congenital and acquired. Most tumors can obstruct the ventricular system and cause hydrocephalus. Removal of the tumor usually resolves the hydrocephalus. In children, hydrocephalus is often part of a genetic syndrome. The primary treatment for hydrocephalus is surgical with a ventricular shunt placement and CSF diversion. ETV and plexectomy are options in certain types of hydrocephalus. Acute hydrocephalus is an emergency.

title: Hydranencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
content: It is extremely important to differentiate between hydranencephaly and severe-extreme hydrocephalus. Both disorders can present similarly, but prognosis and management are very different. A patient with severe-extreme hydrocephalus improves significantly clinically and radiographically after treatment, while those with hydranencephaly do not. If treatment is delayed, it can lead to catastrophic outcomes.

title: Neurosonography Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment -- Congenital Malformations
content: Ventriculomegaly/Hydrocephalus: Dilatation of the ventricles (ventriculomegaly) is called hydrocephalus when associated with increased intraventricular pressure. It can be obstructive, occurring due to obstruction of CSF outflow or impaired absorption of CSF by arachnoid villi or non-obstructive resulting from overproduction of CSF due to choroid plexus papilloma. Obstructive hydrocephalus can be communicating (extraventricular obstruction to CSF flow in subarachnoid space or impaired absorption by arachnoid villi)) or non-communicating (intraventricular obstruction to CSF flow). Congenital causes include aqueductal stenosis, Chiari II malformation, a vein of Galen malformation, Dandy-Walker malformation. The most common hereditary cause of hydrocephalus is X-linked hydrocephalus associated with aqueductal stenosis and occurs as a result of L1CAM mutation. Acquired causes in infants include hemorrhage (most common), infection (usually bacterial meningitis), and tumors. [12]

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

The peritoneal ventricular shunt is a child procedure of the general extracranial ventricular shunt category. content: Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt is a neurosurgical procedural category that is classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية. Within this broader category, the specific procedure Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is identified as a subordinate (child) entity, indicating that it falls under the umbrella of insertion, replacement, or removal activities. title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified within the broader “Brain and Nerves” system and is listed as a member of the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term). It is also linked to the term hlava, indicating a hierarchical relationship that places hydrocephalus under concepts related to the head. The condition is identified in the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, which serves as the primary site where hydrocephalus is found. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي), reflecting its potential impact on cognitive function in older adults. Hydrocephalus frequently co‑occurs with the use of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية), a surgical device that diverts excess cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location. The ventriculoperitoneal shunt is a child concept of the broader category “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is a subcategory of “insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt.” A specific variant of this procedure, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is described as a direct procedure site within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered synonymous or closely related to the general category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Together, these relationships illustrate how hydrocephalus is situated within neuroanatomical and procedural frameworks, its clinical link to cognitive decline, and the central role of various ventricular shunt interventions in its management.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is a central nervous system disorder characterized by abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, a sub‑entity of the nervous system (nervový systém). The condition frequently co‑occurs with a peritoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) and is clinically linked to dementia (خرف) and senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي). Management is performed by neurosurgical (جراحة الأعصاب) procedures that involve insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts, which are classified as ventricular and vascular shunts (تحويلة بطينية وعائية) and fall under the broader scope of neurosurgery. Specific techniques include shunting of the cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site (parent procedure), the peritoneal ventricular shunt (child technique), and a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx (synonymous with the extracranial ventricular shunt), all using the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway as the procedural site. The nervous system also serves as a procedural site for neuro‑muscular interventions (إحصاب عصبي عضلي). Beyond hydrocephalus, other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are associated with disseminated encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور) and lysine protein intolerance (عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين); these entities belong to the broader classification of congenital, familial, and genetic disorders (الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية, SMQ). The SMQ group also encompasses genitourinary congenital anomalies, sexually transmitted infections, infantile paralysis (فاج طفلي), and Stickle syndrome (متلازمة ستيكلر). This classification highlights that hereditary neuro‑degenerative conditions primarily affect the nervous system while the SMQ framework integrates diverse genetic, developmental, and infectious disorders under a unified category.
